parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I was just sitting on the couch earlier today, watching the light fade through the window, and it struck me how quiet everything felt. We were in the same room, but the air between us felt completely heavy and thick. I tried to make a casual joke about the weather, but it just bounced right off the wall. Now I am just staring at the ceiling wondering why such a simple moment felt so incredibly draining. I keep replaying the way the silence stretched out, and it leaves me feeling completely hollow.
turns.model_response:
There is a specific kind of exhaustion that comes from being physically close to someone while feeling miles apart emotionally. It’s often much lonelier than actually being alone.

When you describe the air feeling "heavy and thick," it sounds like you were navigating an invisible barrier. That "hollow" feeling usually happens because you reached out—even with something as small as a joke about the weather—and instead of a connection, you met a void. It takes a lot of emotional energy to try and bridge that gap, and when it doesn't work, the silence that follows feels twice as loud.

It makes sense that you’re staring at the ceiling replaying it. Those "simple" moments are often the most telling; they highlight the state of a relationship more than the big arguments do. 

Are you feeling like this is a new shift in the atmosphere between you two, or has this heavy silence been building up for a while?

turns.user_message: That heavy silence from earlier has been creeping into almost every evening lately, turning what used to be comfortable routines into something exhausting. I find myself carefully measuring my words just to avoid triggering another round of that same cold distance. It feels like I am walking on eggshells in my own living room, constantly second-guessing every little thing I say. The more I try to bridge the gap, the wider it seems to get, leaving me completely drained. I just wish I could remember what it felt like to share a space without feeling so completely alone.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to feel like a stranger in your own home. There is a specific kind of exhaustion that comes from "walking on eggshells"—it’s not just physical; it’s a mental hyper-vigilance that never lets you truly rest.

When you have to measure your words and second-guess your movements, your home stops being a sanctuary and starts feeling like a minefield. That "heavy silence" you described is often louder and more painful than an actual argument because it’s filled with everything that isn't being said. It creates a paradox where you are physically close to someone, yet the emotional distance feels miles wide.

It makes sense that you feel lonely. Being alone is one thing, but feeling alone while sitting right next to someone is a much deeper, sharper kind of isolation. It’s discouraging when your efforts to bridge the gap only seem to push the other person further away, leaving you feeling defeated and empty.

When you think back to those "comfortable routines" you used to have, what is the one thing you miss the most? Is it the ease of conversation, or just the feeling of being truly seen?

turns.user_message: Last night, sitting in that exact same spot on the couch, that familiar quiet finally broke, but it only made everything feel so much worse. I watched them sit across from me, completely checked out, and realized I have no idea who I am talking to anymore. Every attempt to reach out just hits a wall of indifference, and it is starting to eat away at my sense of reality. I am so tired of pretending that this hollow routine is enough, but letting go feels terrifyingly impossible. I just feel completely trapped in a loop of empty gestures and unspoken resentment.
turns.model_response:
That realization—the moment you look at someone you know so well and realize they’ve become a stranger—is a deeply isolating kind of grief. It’s one thing to argue; it’s another thing entirely to be met with a "wall of indifference." Silence and checking out can feel more violent than shouting because they deny you any chance of being seen or heard.

It makes complete sense that this is eating away at your sense of reality. When you are in a partnership, that person often acts as a mirror for your life. When they stop reflecting anything back to you, or when they look through you, it’s natural to start feeling invisible or to question if your needs and feelings are even valid.

The "loop" you described is exhausting because it requires so much emotional labor to maintain the "hollow routine" while receiving nothing in return. You are essentially grieving the relationship while still sitting right in the middle of it.

It’s okay to acknowledge that letting go feels "terrifyingly impossible" right now. You don't have to have the exit strategy figured out tonight. But I want to ask: when you aren't focused on trying to reach them or managing their indifference, what is one small thing you can do just for yourself to feel like *you* are still there? 

Sometimes, when the person across from us stops acknowledging our reality, we have to find ways to anchor ourselves back into it. You aren't crazy for feeling this way, and you aren't wrong for wanting more than a hollow routine.
